source.title: The Roth IRA trade: no break today, every break later

ad_headlines: Pay tax now. Skip it on decades of growth. (Legal — IRC §408A.)
The Roth IRA is the only retirement account the IRS won't touch on the way out.
No deduction today. A tax-free retirement on the back end.
variants.twitter:
The Roth IRA trade, in one line: no deduction this year, every qualified dollar comes out tax-free in retirement — including decades of growth.

Best when you expect your future bracket to be ≥ today's.

Source: IRC §408A, IRS Pub 590-A. Educational, not advice.
variants.linkedin:
A Roth IRA does not give you a tax deduction today. That's the whole trade.

What it gives you instead, per IRC §408A and IRS Publication 590-A:

• The account grows with no tax.
• Qualified withdrawals in retirement come out entirely tax-free — contributions and decades of growth.
• Contributions (not earnings) can generally be pulled back at any time, no penalty, no tax.

Direct-contribution eligibility phases out above certain income levels, which is where the 'backdoor Roth' conversation usually starts (separate topic, separate rules).

The right way to evaluate this isn't 'is the Roth better than the Traditional?' It's 'do I expect my tax bracket in retirement to be the same as, or higher than, my bracket today?' If yes, you're paying tax once at a lower rate. If no, the Traditional often wins.

Either way: it's not a hack. It's the code working as written.

Educational only. Not tax advice — verify income limits and qualified-withdrawal rules against current IRS guidance.
variants.reddit:
**The Roth IRA trade explained without the cheerleading**

A Roth IRA is funded with after-tax dollars. No deduction now. The trade-off the code (IRC §408A) writes you is:

- Tax-free growth.
- Tax-free qualified withdrawals in retirement — contributions AND earnings.
- Contributions (not earnings) can come back out anytime, no tax, no penalty.

When it actually wins: when your future bracket is ≥ your current bracket. If you think you'll be in a lower bracket later, the Traditional IRA often beats it on math alone.

Direct contributions phase out above certain income thresholds; that's where backdoor Roth conversations begin (different rules, separate topic).

Sources: IRS Pub 590-A, IRC §408A. Educational only — not advice. Verify current-year income limits.

brand_voice_principles: Plain-spoken, confident, demystifying. Translates tax-code complexity.
A little irreverent about how needlessly complicated the system is — on the reader's side.
Specific and sourced. Every claim points to a real rule.
No: 'one weird trick', 'IRS hates this', guaranteed savings, sleaze.
